name: Nicaragua
tagline: From the Nicarao and the colonial rival cities of León and Granada to a republic shaped by intervention, revolution, and contested power.
summary: Nicaragua takes its name from the Nicarao, one of the indigenous peoples the Spanish encountered on its Pacific lowlands alongside the Chorotega. From 1524 the conquistador Francisco Hernández de Córdoba founded León and Granada, the rival cities whose Liberal and Conservative elites would dominate national politics for generations, while the Caribbean Mosquito Coast developed separately under British influence. Independence from Spain came on 15 September 1821 and, after the short-lived Federal Republic of Central America, Nicaragua endured the filibuster William Walker, repeated United States intervention, Augusto César Sandino's resistance, and the long Somoza family dictatorship. The Sandinista Revolution of 1979 and the Contra war of the 1980s defined the modern era, followed by a democratic interlude after 1990 and the return and consolidation of power under Daniel Ortega to the present.

founding.label: Independence from Spain
founding.detail: On 15 September 1821 the provinces of the Captaincy General of Guatemala, Nicaragua among them, proclaimed their independence from Spain in the Act of Independence of Central America, signed at Guatemala City without bloodshed. Nicaragua was briefly annexed to the Mexican empire of Agustín de Iturbide, then joined the Federal Republic of Central America in 1823. The federation fractured amid regional rivalry, and Nicaragua withdrew to become a fully separate sovereign state in 1838.

quickFacts.label: Indigenous peoples
quickFacts.value: Nicarao (Nahua) and Chorotega of the Pacific lowlands

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Managua, chosen as a compromise between León and Granada (1852)

eras.title: Indigenous Peoples of the Isthmus
eras.standfirst: Before the Spanish arrival, the Pacific lowlands were home to the Nicarao and Chorotega, peoples linked by migration and trade to the wider civilisations of Mesoamerica and lower Central America.
body: When the Spanish reached western Nicaragua in the early sixteenth century, they found several distinct peoples occupying the fertile Pacific lowlands around the great lakes. The most prominent were the Nicarao, a Nahua-speaking people whose name the country would inherit, and the Chorotega, who spoke a language of the Oto-Manguean family. These agriculturalists cultivated maize, beans, squash, cacao, and other crops, and lived in organised chiefdoms led by caciques. Their presence on the isthmus reflected long centuries of migration that had carried Mesoamerican languages and customs southward into Central America.

The region the Spanish called Greater Nicoya, straddling what is now Pacific Nicaragua and northwestern Costa Rica, was a meeting ground of cultural influences. Its peoples produced fine polychrome pottery and worked in jade and other materials, exchanging goods along routes that connected the civilisations to the north with those of the southern isthmus. Distinct from these lowland societies were the peoples of the eastern rainforests and the Caribbean coast, whose smaller, more dispersed communities followed a different way of life shaped by river and sea.

It was the Nicarao chief encountered by the Spanish, recorded in colonial sources as Nicarao or Nicaragua, who is traditionally said to have given his name to the land and the lake. The first sustained Spanish contact came with the expedition of Gil González Dávila, who reached the Pacific shore in 1522. Within a few years the indigenous polities of the lowlands, weakened by conquest, disease, and the enslavement of their people, would be absorbed into the new colonial order being built around León and Granada.

events.title: Nicarao and Chorotega settle the Pacific lowlands
events.summary: Nahua-speaking Nicarao and Oto-Manguean Chorotega establish chiefdoms in western Nicaragua, farming the volcanic lowlands around Lakes Managua and Nicaragua.

events.title: First Spanish contact
events.summary: The expedition of Gil González Dávila reaches the Pacific coast and encounters the Nicarao, opening the way to Spanish conquest.

pullquote.text: The country takes its name from the Nicarao, the Indigenous people who occupied its Pacific lowlands at the time of the Spanish conquest.
pullquote.attribution: On the origin of the name Nicaragua

eras.title: Conquest, León, and the Mosquito Coast
eras.standfirst: Spain founded the rival cities of León and Granada within the Captaincy General of Guatemala, while the Caribbean Mosquito Coast developed apart under British influence — a colonial division that would mark Nicaragua for centuries.
body: In 1524 the conquistador Francisco Hernández de Córdoba founded the two cities that would define Nicaragua: León, near the Momotombo volcano on Lake Managua, and Granada, on the shore of Lake Nicaragua. León became the colonial capital, the seat of governor and bishop; Granada, with access through the San Juan River to the Caribbean, grew into a wealthy trading port. The original León, abandoned after the Momotombo eruption of 1578 and the earthquake of 1610, survives as the Ruins of León Viejo, inscribed by UNESCO in 2000 as one of the oldest Spanish colonial settlements in the Americas.

For nearly three centuries Nicaragua was governed as a province within the Captaincy General of Guatemala, itself part of the Viceroyalty of New Spain. The colonial economy rested on cattle, indigo, and cacao, worked by an indigenous population devastated by disease and the early slave trade. Society was stratified by caste, and the Roman Catholic Church shaped law, learning, and daily life. The rivalry between aristocratic, conservative Granada and the more liberal, clerical-academic León took root in this era and would dominate the politics of the independent republic.

The Caribbean lowlands followed a wholly separate course. From the seventeenth century Great Britain cultivated an alliance with the Miskito people, recognising a line of Miskito kings and treating the Mosquito Coast as a protectorate beyond effective Spanish control. English and later British settlers, traders, and buccaneers operated along this shore, and the region's Protestant, Anglophone, and Afro-indigenous character set it apart from the Hispanic, Catholic Pacific. The Mosquito Coast would not be fully incorporated into Nicaragua until 1894.

events.title: Founding of León and Granada
events.summary: Francisco Hernández de Córdoba founds León and Granada, the rival cities whose Liberal and Conservative elites would shape Nicaraguan politics for generations.

events.title: León Viejo abandoned
events.summary: The original León, struck by the Momotombo eruption of 1578 and the earthquake of 1610, is abandoned; its buried ruins later become Nicaragua's first UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: British protectorate over the Mosquito Coast
events.summary: Great Britain allies with the Miskito kings and treats the Caribbean Mosquito Coast as a protectorate, leaving the region distinct from Hispanic Pacific Nicaragua.

figures.name: Francisco Hernández de Córdoba
figures.blurb: Conquistador who in 1524 founded León and Granada, the two cities at the heart of colonial and republican Nicaragua. The national currency, the córdoba, bears his name.

pullquote.text: León Viejo is one of the oldest Spanish colonial settlements in the Americas… outstanding testimony to the social and economic structures of the Spanish Empire in the 16th century.

eras.title: Independence, Rivalry, and the Filibuster
eras.standfirst: Independence in 1821 gave way to the bitter León–Granada feud, the chosen compromise capital of Managua, and the extraordinary episode of the American filibuster William Walker, who briefly made himself president of Nicaragua.
body: Nicaragua won its independence from Spain peacefully on 15 September 1821, as part of the general Act of Independence of Central America. After a brief union with Mexico, it joined the Federal Republic of Central America in 1823, but the federation was wracked by the same divisions that beset the country itself, and Nicaragua declared full separation in 1838. The new republic was almost immediately consumed by the rivalry between the Liberals of León and the Conservatives of Granada, whose contest for power repeatedly spilled into civil war. To dampen the feud, the lakeside town of Managua, lying between the two, was designated the capital in 1852.

It was this rivalry that opened the door to one of the strangest chapters in Latin American history. In 1855 the Liberal faction of León hired the American adventurer William Walker, who sailed from California with a band of armed followers known as filibusters. Walker's men captured Granada, and in 1856 he had himself installed as president of Nicaragua; his regime was briefly recognised by the United States. Walker reintroduced slavery and made English an official language, but his ambitions alarmed Cornelius Vanderbilt and the neighbouring Central American states, which combined to drive him out. He surrendered in 1857, returned to the region, and was executed in Honduras in 1860.

The shock of the Walker episode produced a rare interval of unity, and Conservative governments based in Granada held power for three decades of relative stability known as the thirty years. In 1860 the Treaty of Managua transferred British claims on the Caribbean to Nicaraguan sovereignty as a self-governing Mosquito Reserve, though full integration remained incomplete. The era closed in 1893, when the Liberal general José Santos Zelaya seized power, ending Conservative dominance and inaugurating a forceful modernising regime.

events.title: Independence from Spain
events.summary: Nicaragua, with the other provinces of the Captaincy General of Guatemala, declares independence from Spain in the Act of Independence of Central America.

events.title: Separation from the Central American federation
events.summary: Amid regional rivalry, Nicaragua withdraws from the Federal Republic of Central America to become a fully sovereign state.

events.title: William Walker's filibuster
events.summary: Hired by León's Liberals, the American filibuster William Walker seizes Granada and makes himself president of Nicaragua in 1856 before a coalition of Central American states expels him.

events.title: Treaty of Managua
events.summary: Britain cedes its claims on the Caribbean coast to Nicaraguan sovereignty, creating a self-governing Mosquito Reserve.

figures.name: William Walker
figures.blurb: Tennessee adventurer who led a mercenary expedition into Nicaragua's civil war, seized Granada, and ruled as president in 1856–57. Driven out by a Central American coalition, he was executed in Honduras in 1860.

pullquote.text: Walker went to Nicaragua in 1855 as the leader of a mercenary army… and in July 1856 set himself up as the country's president.

eras.title: Intervention, Sandino, and the Somoza Dynasty
eras.standfirst: Decades of United States intervention and Marine occupation provoked the nationalist resistance of Augusto César Sandino — and, after his murder, the rise of the Somoza family, whose dictatorship endured for more than four decades.
body: The Liberal moderniser José Santos Zelaya, in power from 1893, finally incorporated the Mosquito Coast into Nicaragua in 1894 and pursued an assertive policy that brought him into conflict with the United States, then keen to control any future interoceanic canal route. Washington helped engineer his fall in 1909, and from 1912 United States Marines were stationed in Nicaragua, propping up Conservative governments and overseeing the country's finances for most of the next two decades. This long intervention bred deep resentment of foreign tutelage.

From 1927 the nationalist Augusto César Sandino refused to accept a US-brokered settlement and waged a guerrilla war against the Marines and the new National Guard from the mountains of the north. His success in eluding capture made him a hero across Latin America as a symbol of resistance to American power. The Marines withdrew in 1933, leaving the National Guard under its commander Anastasio Somoza García. In 1934, after talks in Managua, Sandino was lured to a meeting and assassinated by Guard officers — a killing that cleared Somoza's path to power.

In 1936 Somoza García deposed the elected president and took the office for himself, founding a dynasty that would dominate Nicaragua until 1979. Through the National Guard, control of the economy, and a pliant political system, the family amassed vast wealth and held power directly or through proxies. After the founder's assassination in 1956, his sons Luis and then Anastasio Somoza Debayle succeeded him. Mounting corruption, the diversion of aid after the catastrophic Managua earthquake of 1972, and brutal repression turned the country against the regime and swelled support for the Sandinista insurgency that bore Sandino's name.

events.title: Incorporation of the Mosquito Coast
events.summary: President José Santos Zelaya militarily incorporates the British-influenced Caribbean coast into the Nicaraguan state.

events.title: United States Marine occupation
events.summary: US Marines are stationed in Nicaragua to support Conservative governments and oversee its finances, with only a brief interruption, until 1933.

events.title: Sandino's resistance
events.summary: Augusto César Sandino wages guerrilla war against the Marines and National Guard, becoming a hemispheric symbol of resistance to US intervention.

events.title: Assassination of Sandino
events.summary: Sandino is lured to Managua and murdered by National Guard officers under Anastasio Somoza García, opening the way to the Somoza dictatorship.

events.title: Rise of the Somoza dynasty
events.summary: Anastasio Somoza García seizes the presidency, founding a family dictatorship that, directly or through proxies, rules Nicaragua until 1979.

figures.name: Augusto César Sandino
figures.blurb: Led an armed resistance against US Marines and the National Guard from 1927. Assassinated in 1934 on the orders of Somoza García, he became the namesake and inspiration of the later Sandinista movement.

figures.name: Anastasio Somoza García
figures.blurb: National Guard commander who seized power in 1936 and ruled Nicaragua directly or through chosen presidents until his assassination in 1956, founding a dynasty that lasted until 1979.

pullquote.text: In Nicaragua he became a popular hero and gave his name to the Sandinistas.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopædia Britannica, on César Augusto Sandino

eras.title: Revolution and the Contra War
eras.standfirst: The Sandinista Revolution of 1979 toppled the Somoza dictatorship and set out to remake Nicaraguan society; the United States–backed Contra war that followed turned the country into a Cold War battleground until the election of 1990.
body: In July 1979 the Sandinista National Liberation Front (FSLN) entered Managua as Anastasio Somoza Debayle fled, ending more than four decades of family rule. The insurrection that brought them to power is estimated to have cost on the order of fifty thousand lives. The new Sandinista government, with Daniel Ortega emerging as its leading figure, launched ambitious programmes of land reform, a celebrated national literacy crusade, and expanded health care, while aligning itself with Cuba and the Soviet bloc. Its measures and its Marxist orientation alarmed Washington and divided Nicaraguan society.

From the early 1980s the United States, under President Ronald Reagan, financed and armed a counter-revolutionary insurgency known as the Contras, drawn in part from former National Guardsmen and operating from Honduras and Costa Rica. The ensuing war ravaged the rural economy and population through the decade. Estimates of the dead vary considerably and should be treated with caution; figures commonly cited range broadly into the tens of thousands. The conflict also entangled the United States in the Iran–Contra affair and in a 1986 International Court of Justice judgment against its support for the Contras.

Pressure for a settlement grew at home and abroad. The Central American presidents signed the Esquipulas II peace accord in August 1987, committing to democratisation and an end to outside support for insurgencies. In a result that surprised many observers, the 1990 presidential election was won not by Ortega but by Violeta Barrios de Chamorro, candidate of a broad opposition coalition. Ortega accepted defeat and handed over power — a peaceful transfer that brought the war to a close and opened a new democratic chapter.

events.title: Sandinista Revolution
events.summary: The FSLN overthrows Anastasio Somoza Debayle, ending the dynasty; the insurrection is estimated to have cost about 50,000 lives.

events.title: The Contra war
events.summary: The United States finances and arms the Contra insurgency against the Sandinista government; the war kills an estimated tens of thousands and devastates the economy. Casualty figures vary and are estimates.

events.title: Esquipulas II peace accord
events.summary: Central American presidents sign a regional peace plan committing to democratisation and an end to support for insurgencies.

events.title: Chamorro defeats Ortega
events.summary: Violeta Barrios de Chamorro wins the presidency for an opposition coalition; Ortega cedes power in a peaceful transfer that ends the war.

figures.name: Daniel Ortega
figures.blurb: Member of the FSLN junta that took power in 1979 and elected president in 1984. Defeated in 1990, he returned to office in 2007 and consolidated control over the state in the years that followed.

figures.name: Violeta Barrios de Chamorro
figures.blurb: Newspaper publisher's widow and opposition candidate whose 1990 victory ended Sandinista rule and the Contra war, making her the first woman elected president in the Americas.

pullquote.text: The Sandinista-dominated government was finally defeated by the U.S.-funded National Opposition Union… in the 1990 presidential elections.

eras.title: Democratic Interlude and Ortega's Return
eras.standfirst: After a decade and a half of multiparty government, Daniel Ortega returned to the presidency in 2007 and steadily consolidated power, a course that culminated in the deadly crisis of 2018 and a tightly controlled state.
body: The governments of Violeta Chamorro and her successors, Arnoldo Alemán and Enrique Bolaños, presided over a difficult but peaceful period of reconstruction, demobilisation, and market reform through the 1990s and early 2000s, even as poverty and the legacy of the war weighed heavily on the country. Ortega, having lost successive elections, rebuilt the FSLN and forged new alliances, and in 2006 he won the presidency once more, returning to office in January 2007.

Back in power, Ortega gradually concentrated authority. A 2009 court ruling and later constitutional changes removed limits on re-election, the FSLN tightened its grip on the electoral apparatus and institutions, and Ortega's wife, Rosario Murillo, rose to become vice-president and a central figure in the government. In April 2018 protests against social-security cuts grew into a nationwide movement against the government, which responded with force. Human rights bodies, including the United Nations and the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights, documented hundreds of deaths; estimates of the toll generally range upward of three hundred and should be regarded as approximate.

In the years after 2018 the government detained or exiled opposition figures, closed civic organisations and independent media, and stripped citizenship from prominent critics. Ortega was returned in the 2021 election, held after leading rivals had been jailed, a contest widely criticised abroad. Into the mid-2020s Nicaragua remained one of the poorer countries of the Americas, its politics dominated by Ortega and Murillo and its course a subject of sustained international concern, even as the deep historical fault lines of region, ideology, and foreign involvement endured.

events.title: Ortega returns to power
events.summary: Having won the 2006 election, Daniel Ortega is inaugurated president again, beginning a long second period in office.

events.title: Re-election limits removed
events.summary: A 2009 court ruling and 2014 constitutional reform abolish term limits, enabling Ortega's continued re-election and the consolidation of FSLN control.

events.title: The 2018 crisis
events.summary: Protests against social-security cuts become a mass anti-government movement met with deadly force; the UN and IACHR document a death toll estimated upward of 300 (figures are estimates).

events.title: Contested re-election
events.summary: Ortega wins a further term after leading opposition candidates are detained, in an election widely criticised internationally.

figures.name: Rosario Murillo
figures.blurb: Wife of Daniel Ortega and a dominant figure in his government, she became vice-president in 2017 and a central architect of the post-2018 consolidation of power.

pullquote.text: The Sandinistas returned to power in 2007, having promised to uphold many of the economic reforms of their predecessors.

figures.name: Augusto César Sandino
figures.blurb: Led the resistance to US Marine occupation from 1927 until his assassination in 1934; his name became the banner of the revolutionary movement that seized power in 1979.

figures.name: Anastasio Somoza García
figures.blurb: Took power in 1936 through command of the National Guard, founding a family dynasty that dominated Nicaragua for more than four decades.

figures.name: Daniel Ortega
figures.blurb: Revolutionary junta member and president (1985–90, 2007– ) whose return to power and subsequent consolidation of control have defined twenty-first-century Nicaragua.

sources.label: Territorial Expansion, Filibustering, and U.S. Interest in Central America
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
